title: Home Office Ergonomics
subtitle: Correct desk height, monitor position, lighting. Prevention of carpal tunnel syndrome and back pain
description: This checklist is a comprehensive guide to setting up a home workspace from the perspective of biomechanics and physiology. You'll learn how incorrect computer posture triggers a cascade of problems from chronic pain to carpal tunnel syndrome, and get precise, scientifically grounded parameters for adjusting your desk, chair, monitor, and lighting. Investing in ergonomics is an investment in productivity and long-term health.

items.title: The Foundation: Desk Height and Elbow Position
items.source: ANSI/HFES 100-2007 — Human Factors Engineering of Computer Workstations
items.text: The key parameter from which ergonomics begins is the elbow joint angle. When working with a keyboard and mouse, forearms should be parallel to the floor, and the angle between the upper arm and forearm should be 90-100 degrees. If the desk is too high, you raise your shoulders — engaging the trapezius muscles, which are not designed for prolonged static load. Result: chronic tension in the neck and shoulders, tension headaches. If the desk is too low, you slouch, overloading the thoracic spine. The standard office desk height of 72-75 cm is suitable for people 170-180 cm tall. If your height is outside this range, your desk should be height-adjustable.
items.tip: Sit on a chair, let your arms hang freely, bend your elbows to 90 degrees. Measure the distance from the floor to your elbow. That is your ideal desk height.

items.title: Monitor Position: Saving the Cervical Spine
items.text: Incorrect monitor position is the main cause of 'tech neck'. An adult's head weighs 4.5-5.5 kg. Tilting forward by 15 degrees increases the load on the cervical spine to 12 kg; tilting by 45 degrees — to 22 kg. Chronic overload leads to early osteochondrosis, disc protrusions, and cervical herniations. The gold standard: the top edge of the screen should be at or slightly below eye level. Distance to monitor — arm's length (50-70 cm). If you work on a laptop without an external monitor, you are guaranteed to violate this rule.
items.tip: Place your laptop on a stand to raise the screen to eye level, and use an external keyboard and mouse. This is the minimum investment in neck health.

items.title: Anatomy of Correct Sitting
items.text: Professor Stuart McGill, a world expert on spine biomechanics, formulates three key rules for sitting. First: the pelvis should be in a neutral position, not tilted back. This requires lumbar support that maintains the natural lordosis. Second: knees should be at or slightly below hip level, feet flat on the floor. Third: don't sit still. The spine is not designed for prolonged static load. Ideal sitting is dynamic sitting with micro-movements and changes in posture every 20-30 minutes. Even an ideal chair won't save you if you sit on it for 8 hours without moving.
items.tip: If your chair lacks lumbar support, roll up a small towel and place it behind your lower back. Check: when sitting, there should be 2-3 finger widths between the back of your knee and the edge of the seat.

items.title: Feet and Support: Why Legs Shouldn't Dangle
items.text: A frequently ignored aspect of ergonomics is leg position. If feet don't reach the floor, the weight of your legs pulls the front of the pelvis down, causing the lower back to round. This triggers a chain reaction: rounded lower back → hunched shoulders → forward head posture. Additionally, pressure under the knees from the seat edge impairs blood and lymph circulation. Solution: either lower the chair (if desk height permits) or use a footrest. The ankle angle should be 90-100 degrees, feet fully supported.
items.tip: If buying a footrest, choose one with a slight tilt and massage surface. This stimulates micro-movements of the feet and improves circulation.

items.title: Carpal Tunnel Syndrome Prevention: Mouse and Keyboard
items.source: Journal of Hand Surgery — Carpal Tunnel Syndrome Prevention
items.text: Carpal tunnel syndrome is compression of the median nerve in the narrow anatomical wrist canal. Cause: prolonged unnatural hand position — wrist extension while typing or holding a mouse with the palm twisted outward. First signs: numbness of the thumb, index, and middle fingers at night, tingling, hand weakness. Prevention: 1) wrist should be straight, no bend (use a gel wrist rest in front of the keyboard), 2) mouse should rest in the palm, not be gripped by the fingers, 3) vertical mouse reduces forearm pronation.
items.tip: Every 30 minutes do a simple exercise: extend your arm in front of you palm up, with the other hand gently pull your fingers down toward the floor, stretching the wrist flexors. Hold for 15 seconds. Switch hands.

items.title: Lighting: Color Temperature and Brightness
items.text: Workspace lighting directly affects visual fatigue, circadian rhythms, and productivity. Key parameters: 1) Color temperature. For work in the first half of the day, cool white light (4000-5000K) is optimal — it suppresses melatonin and increases concentration. After 4 PM, switch to warm light (2700-3000K) to avoid disrupting circadian rhythms. 2) Illuminance of work surface: 300-500 lux for general tasks, 500-750 lux for document work. 3) No glare on the screen. The monitor should not face a window or reflect light fixtures.
items.tip: Use a desk lamp with adjustable color temperature and brightness. Light should come from the side of your non-dominant hand to avoid casting shadows on documents.

items.title: The 20-20-20 Rule and Visual Hygiene
items.text: Prolonged focusing at close range overstrains the ciliary muscle of the eye, causing accommodation spasm and computer vision syndrome (dryness, burning, blurring). The 20-20-20 rule: every 20 minutes of screen work, look at an object at least 20 feet (6 meters) away for 20 seconds. This allows the ciliary muscle to relax. A second important factor: blink rate. When working at a computer, it drops from 15-20 to 5-7 times per minute, leading to corneal drying.
items.tip: Install a reminder program (EyeLeo, Workrave). Keep preservative-free artificial tears on your desk. Drink enough water — dehydration reduces tear production.

items.title: Standing Desks: Myths and Reality
items.source: Journal of Physical Activity and Health — Sit-Stand Desk Meta-Analysis
items.text: Height-adjustable desks (sit-stand desks) have become mainstream, but it's important to understand: standing work is not a panacea. Meta-analyses show prolonged standing carries its own risks: varicose veins, stress on leg joints, fatigue. Optimal mode is alternation: 25-30 minutes sitting, 5-10 minutes standing, 2-3 minutes walking. An anti-fatigue mat when standing reduces stress on feet and spine. When standing, maintain a neutral spine, don't lean on one leg, and don't lock your knees in hyperextension.
items.tip: If you have a regular desk, use a high bar stool or windowsill as an impromptu standing desk for 15-20 minutes per hour. This already provides the necessary posture change.

items.title: Micro-breaks and Office Exercises
items.text: Even perfect ergonomics cannot replace movement. The spine is nourished through diffusion, which occurs only with load changes. Professor McGill developed the 'Big 3' exercises for back health that can be done in the office: 1) Cat-camel (spinal mobilization), 2) Side plank (strengthening the quadratus lumborum), 3) Bird-dog (stabilizing deep back muscles). Also effective: heel raises to prevent venous stasis in the legs, shoulder rolls, neck side bends.
items.tip: Set a timer for every 45-50 minutes. Stand up, do 2-3 exercises from the 'Big 3' or simply walk to the kitchen for a glass of water. This is not a break from work, but an investment in productivity.

items.title: Personalization: A Self-Assessment Checklist
items.source: Cornell University Ergonomics Web — Self-Assessment Tool
items.text: Ergonomics is not a set of universal rules, but an individual adjustment to a specific person's anthropometry. Cornell University developed a workplace self-assessment protocol. Key checkpoints: 1) Top edge of monitor at eye level (check with a ruler). 2) Elbows at 90 degrees, forearms on armrests or desk without raising shoulders. 3) Lower back supported, knees at or slightly below hip level. 4) Feet flat on floor or footrest. 5) Wrists straight, no upward bend. 6) No glare on screen. 7) Documents on a stand between keyboard and monitor to avoid turning your head.
items.tip: Take a profile photo of yourself at your desk. Check all 7 points against the checklist. Ask someone to photograph you when you're not posing — your real working posture often differs from the 'correct' one.

quiz.q: What elbow joint angle is considered optimal when working at a keyboard?
At what level should the top edge of the monitor be?
a: At or slightly below eye level

quiz.q: What load does the cervical spine experience when the head is tilted forward 45 degrees?
What is an early sign of carpal tunnel syndrome?
a: Nighttime numbness of the thumb, index, and middle fingers

quiz.q: What is the optimal color temperature for work in the first half of the day?
